attributes.stem:
A 47-year-old man presents to the General Practice clinic with recurrent bouts of dark-coloured stool which have been occurring over the last 12 months. The last episode was one week ago. He reports fatigue and lethargy. He has no significant medical history and takes no regular medications.

On examination, he appears well. His pulse rate is 97/min, blood pressure 110/75 mmHg, respiratory rate 16/min, SpO2 98% on room air, and temperature 36.9°C. Endoscopy and colonoscopy have both been normal and each repeated on two occasions. His lab results are shown below:

Haemoglobin = 102 g/L (135-180)
MCV = 71 fL (80-100)
WBC = 7.8 x 10^9/L (4.0-11.0)
Platelets = 175 x 10^9/L (150-400)
attributes.stemQuestion: Which one of the following is the most appropriate next step in management?
attributes.explanation:
This patient's history with bouts of dark-coloured stools (melaena) and lab results showing anaemia and low MCV (most likely due to iron deficiency anaemia) raise the suspicion for upper gastrointestinal bleeding (UGIB). All haemodynamically stable patients with suspicion for UGIB require visual confirmation of the gastrointestinal (GI) lesions with endoscopy. Patients who have an inconclusive endoscopy are further evaluated with colonoscopy. If both endoscopy and colonoscopy are inconclusive, both of these tests need to be repeated. If the results are still negative, consider evaluation for small bowel bleeding with video capsule endoscopy. This is because a standard endoscope and a colonoscope are unable to visualise the small intestines. The patient swallows a pill-shaped camera that displays the insides of the whole GI tract.

Push enteroscopy involves the use of a push enteroscope or a paediatric colonoscope that is "pushed" past the ligament of Trietz for better visualisation of small intestines. It is an invasive procedure compared to capsule endoscopy and is usually indicated if capsule endoscopy and CT/MR enteroclysis are equivocal and there is recurrent or an overt bleeding episode. It can also be used to target and treat a specific lesion detected on capsule endoscopy.

CT and MR enteroclysis are indicated if the video capsule endoscopy is inconclusive and the bleeding continues. Video capsule endoscopy is preferred in this patient because its diagnostic accuracy exceeds that of CT or MR enteroclysis.

A CT angiogram might be appropriate if the patient was experiencing significant active bleeding, in which case an arterial blush may be detected. In this case, the bleeding appears to have stopped or blood is being lost at a very slow rate, and a CT angiogram is unlikely to detect this low level of bleeding.
learningPoints: Capsule endoscopy has an important role to play in the detection of bleeding points in the small intestine.
Video capsule endoscopy is preferred over CT or MR enteroclysis for investigating suspected small bowel bleeding when upper endoscopy and colonoscopy are normal.
references: Chetcuti Zammit, Stefania, and Reena Sidhu. "Capsule endoscopy–recent developments and future directions." Expert review of gastroenterology & hepatology 15.2 (2021): 127-137.

Hakimian S, Jawaid S, Guilarte-Walker Y, Mathew J, Cave D. Video capsule endoscopy as a tool for evaluation of obscure overt gastrointestinal bleeding in the intensive care unit. Endosc Int Open. 2018 Aug;6(8):E989-E993. doi: 10.1055/a-0590-3940.

Schlag C, Menzel C, Nennstiel S, Neu B, Phillip V, Schuster T, Schmid RM, von Delius S. Emergency video capsule endoscopy in patients with acute severe GI bleeding and negative upper endoscopy results. Gastrointest Endosc. 2015 Apr;81(4):889-95. doi: 10.1016/j.gie.2014.09.035.
